title: Durham Castle

infobox.value: is a [Norman](/wiki/Norman_architecture "Norman architecture") castle in the city of [Durham, England](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England"), which has been occupied since 1837 by [University College, Durham](/wiki/University_College,_Durham "University College, Durham") after its previous role as the residence of the Bishops of Durham. Designated since 1986 as a [cultural World Heritage Site in England](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of World Heritage Sites in the United Kingdom"), along with [Durham Cathedral](/wiki/Durham_Cathedral "Durham Cathedral"), the castle is open to the general public to visit, but only through guided tours, since it is in use as a working building and is home to over 100 students. The castle stands on top of a hill above the [River Wear](/wiki/River_Wear "River Wear") on Durham's [peninsula](/wiki/Peninsula "Peninsula"), opposite [Durham Cathedral](/wiki/Durham_Cathedral "Durham Cathedral") ([grid reference](/wiki/Ordnance_Survey_National_Grid "Ordnance Survey National Grid") [NZ274423](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Durham_Castle&params=54.774875_N_1.575580_W_region:GB_scale:25000)).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Norman castle in Durham, England

Durham Castle

| [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site") | |

| [Coordinates](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system") | [54°46′29″N 1°34′34″W﻿ / ﻿54.77472°N 1.57611°W﻿ / 54.77472; -1.57611](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Durham_Castle&params=54_46_29_N_1_34_34_W_) |
| [Durham Castle is located in County Durham](/wiki/File:Durham_UK_relief_location_map.jpg "Durham Castle is located in County Durham") Durham Castle  Durham Castle  Location of Durham Castle in County Durham | |

**Durham Castle** is a [Norman](/wiki/Norman_architecture "Norman architecture") castle in the city of [Durham, England](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England"), which has been occupied since 1837 by [University College, Durham](/wiki/University_College,_Durham "University College, Durham") after its previous role as the residence of the Bishops of Durham. Designated since 1986 as a [cultural World Heritage Site in England](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of World Heritage Sites in the United Kingdom"), along with [Durham Cathedral](/wiki/Durham_Cathedral "Durham Cathedral"), the castle is open to the general public to visit, but only through guided tours, since it is in use as a working building and is home to over 100 students. The castle stands on top of a hill above the [River Wear](/wiki/River_Wear "River Wear") on Durham's [peninsula](/wiki/Peninsula "Peninsula"), opposite [Durham Cathedral](/wiki/Durham_Cathedral "Durham Cathedral") ([grid reference](/wiki/Ordnance_Survey_National_Grid "Ordnance Survey National Grid") [NZ274423](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Durham_Castle&params=54.774875_N_1.575580_W_region:GB_scale:25000)).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_Castle&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_Castle&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early history")]

Construction of the castle, of the [motte and bailey](/wiki/Motte-and-bailey_castle "Motte-and-bailey castle") design favoured by the Normans, began in 1072 under the orders of [William the Conqueror](/wiki/William_the_Conqueror "William the Conqueror"), six years after the [Norman conquest of England](/wiki/Norman_conquest_of_England "Norman conquest of England"), and soon after the Normans first came to the North. The construction took place under the supervision of [Waltheof, Earl of Northumbria](/wiki/Waltheof,_Earl_of_Northumbria "Waltheof, Earl of Northumbria"), until he rebelled against William and was executed in 1076.[[1]](#cite_note-whs-1) Stone for the new buildings was cut from the cliffs below the walls and moved up using winches.[[2]](#cite_note-Durham_of_the_Prince_Bishops-2)

The holder of the office of the [bishop of Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham "Bishop of Durham"), Bishop [Walcher](/wiki/Walcher "Walcher") at the time, was appointed by the king to exercise royal authority on his behalf, with the castle being his seat.[[1]](#cite_note-whs-1) Adding to his status by the purchase of the [Earldom of Northumbria](/wiki/Earl_of_Northumbria "Earl of Northumbria"), Walcher began a line of [prince-bishops](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham#History "Bishop of Durham") which continued until the Bishops of Durham lost their temporal powers under the [Durham (County Palatine) Act 1836](/wiki/Durham_(County_Palatine)_Act_1836 "Durham (County Palatine) Act 1836").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The prince-bishops were entitled to raise an army, levy taxes and mint their own coinage. The wide remit granted them by the English crown allowed them virtual autonomy to act as a defence both against Scottish incursions from the north, and internal rebellions.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Walcher continued the building of the castle, as a combination of bishops' palace and defensive stronghold. Inner and outer baileys and a keep were constructed. The earliest structures may have been of wood, but there is evidence of building in stone from the end of the 11th century.[[5]](#cite_note-unesco-5) In May 1080, the castle was attacked and besieged for four days by rebels from Northumbria and Bishop Walcher was killed.

In the 12th century, Bishop Pudsey ([Hugh de Puiset](/wiki/Hugh_de_Puiset "Hugh de Puiset")) built the Norman archway and the [Galilee](/wiki/Galilee_(church_architecture) "Galilee (church architecture)") of the cathedral.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In 1177, King [Henry II of England](/wiki/Henry_II_of_England "Henry II of England") seized the castle after a disagreement with de Puiset.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Other major alterations were made by Bishop [Thomas Hatfield](/wiki/Thomas_Hatfield "Thomas Hatfield") in the 1300s, including a rebuilding of the keep and enlargement of the keep mount.[[8]](#cite_note-Page_1928_64–91-8)

The castle has a large Great Hall, originally called a Dining Hall, created by Bishop [Antony Bek](/wiki/Antony_Bek_(bishop_of_Durham) "Antony Bek (bishop of Durham)") in the early 14th century; Bishop Hatfield added a wooden minstrels' gallery. The hall was modified and enlarged, then reduced, in size by subsequent bishops.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) Today, the Hall is 14 metres (46 ft) high and over 30 metres (98 ft) long.[[8]](#cite_note-Page_1928_64–91-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Durham_Castle%2C_April_2017_%2818%29_%2833687759022%29.jpg/500px-Durham_Castle%2C_April_2017_%2818%29_%2833687759022%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Durham_Castle,_April_2017_(18)_(33687759022).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Durham_Castle%2C_April_2017_%2812%29_%2833843774815%29.jpg/500px-Durham_Castle%2C_April_2017_%2812%29_%2833843774815%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Durham_Castle,_April_2017_(12)_(33843774815).jpg)

Entrance to Bishop Bek's Great Hall and interior view

### University College

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_Castle&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: University College")]

Main article: [University College, Durham](/wiki/University_College,_Durham "University College, Durham")

The Castle remained the bishop's palace for the [Bishop of Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham "Bishop of Durham") until [Auckland Castle](/wiki/Auckland_Castle "Auckland Castle") was made the bishops' residence in 1832; the current bishop still maintains offices at that castle, roughly ten miles to the south. Subsequently, Durham castle was donated to the University of Durham[[2]](#cite_note-Durham_of_the_Prince_Bishops-2) by Bishop [William Van Mildert](/wiki/William_Van_Mildert "William Van Mildert") and would later become the [college](/wiki/University_College,_Durham "University College, Durham").[[11]](#cite_note-11) The college did not occupy the castle until 1837, after the next Bishop, [Edward Maltby](/wiki/Edward_Maltby "Edward Maltby"), had completed renovations of the building.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The cathedral was targeted for a [Baedeker Blitz](/wiki/Baedeker_Blitz "Baedeker Blitz") or bombing raid by Germany but escaped because fog rolled in and blocked the pilots' view.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Architecture and description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_Castle&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Architecture and description")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Durham_Castle_from_the_courtyard.jpg/960px-Durham_Castle_from_the_courtyard.jpg)](/wiki/File:Durham_Castle_from_the_courtyard.jpg "Durham Castle from the courtyard")

Durham Castle from the courtyard

### Chapels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_Castle&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Chapels")]

The Norman Chapel is the oldest accessible part of the castle built about 1078. Its architecture is [Anglian](/wiki/Anglo-Saxons "Anglo-Saxons") in nature, possibly due to forced Anglian labour being used to build it. In the 15th century, its three windows were all but blocked up because of the expanded keep. It fell into disuse until 1841 when it was used as a corridor through which to access the keep. During the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), it was used as a command and observation post for the [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force"). The chapel was re-consecrated shortly after the war and is still used for weekly services by the college.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Tunstall's Chapel, named after the [Bishop of Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham "Bishop of Durham") [Cuthbert Tunstall](/wiki/Cuthbert_Tunstall "Cuthbert Tunstall"), was built in the 16th century and is used for worship within the college.[[15]](#cite_note-15) It was modified in the 17th century by Bishop Cosin.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## World Heritage status and historic listing designations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_Castle&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: World Heritage status and historic listing designations")]

Durham Castle is jointly designated a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site") with Durham Cathedral, a short distance across [Palace Green](/wiki/Palace_Green "Palace Green").[[1]](#cite_note-whs-1)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") report provides specifics about the Castle's important aspects:[[18]](#cite_note-18)

> Within the Castle precinct are later buildings of the Durham Palatinate, reflecting the Prince-Bishops’ civic responsibilities and privileges. These include the Bishop’s Court (now a library), almshouses, and schools. Palace Green, a large open space connecting the various buildings of the site once provided the Prince Bishops with a venue for processions and gatherings befitting their status, and is now still a forum for public events.

Seventeen elements of the castle are [listed](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building"), all at the highest Grade, I. These are: the keep, the north and west ranges, the entrance gatehouse, a bastion and twelve sections of the castle walls.[[19]](#cite_note-19)
